context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 10

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 10


Problem 10.1 (20 pts)

A point source of light, S, of frequency ν is placed in the air (n = 1) a distance L from a perfect conductor as shown in Figure 1.

[Figure 1: Point light source — a source S a distance L from a vertical perfect conductor (mirror), with an observer far away in the direction perpendicular to the conductor.]

(a) For what values of L will the observer see a maximum in the interference pattern a long distance from S in a direction perpendicular to the perfect conductor? (You may ignore any absorption by S of the reflected light.)
(b) For what values of L will the observer see a minimum in the interference pattern a long distance from S in a direction perpendicular to the perfect conductor? (You may ignore any absorption by S of the reflected light.)
(c) What is the minimum thickness of glass, of refractive index n, which must be placed between the light source and the mirror, so that for a given L a minimum replaces a maximum, and vice versa? (You may assume there is no reflection from the glass surface.)


Problem 10.2 (20 pts)

Three long narrow slits shown in Figure 2 are illuminated by a monochromatic source of light with wavelength λ = 500 nanometers (1 nm = 10^(−9) m). The distance between slit centers is d = 1 millimeter. The transmitted light is projected on a screen L = 5 meters away. Assume that the slits are very narrow such that the single slit diffraction can be ignored.

[Figure 2: Three slit screen — three slits spaced 1 mm apart, with a screen 5 m away (vertical and horizontal dimensions are not to scale).]

Consider first a case when the top slit is completely blocked.
(a) What is the distance in millimeters between the central intensity maximum (fringe) and the first minimum on the screen?
(b) Sketch the intensity distribution near the central fringe including a total of 5 neighboring intensity maxima (central and two on each side). Label carefully the axes. What is the maximum intensity of each of the 5 fringes if the single slit intensity is I0?

The top slit is now uncovered.
(c) What is now the distance in millimeters between the central maximum (fringe) and the first minimum on the screen?
(d) Sketch the new intensity distribution near the central fringe including a total of 5 neighboring maxima (central and two on each side) as a function of distance from the center of the central peak x. Label carefully the axes.
(e) What is the maximum intensity of each of the 5 fringes?

The top slit is now covered with a magic material that transmits only half of the incident light intensity without affecting phase, polarization or any other properties of light.
(f) What is the maximum intensity that a fringe can have in this configuration? Which of the 5 neighboring fringes will have this intensity?
(g) Write an equation in terms of fringe position x that can be solved to find the position of the intensity minimum nearest to the central peak. Do not solve.
(h) Sketch now the new intensity distribution near the central fringe including a total of 5 neighboring maxima (central and two on each side).


Problem 10.3 (20 pts)

Two dipole radiators are separated by a distance λ/2 along the x-axis. The dipoles are oriented along z. Assume r ≫ λ. See Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Two dipoles separated by λ/2 along the x-axis, oriented along z; the observation direction r makes an angle Θ in the x-y plane.]

(a) Find the relative intensities of the radiation in the x−y plane at θ = 0, π/3, π/2, and π if the oscillations of the dipoles are in phase.
(b) Repeat (a) if the oscillators are out of phase by π.
(c) The oscillators are spaced by a distance λ/4 and they are π/2 out of phase. Find the relative intensities at θ = 0, π/2, 3π/2.


Problem 10.4 (20 pts)

A small amount of oil (n = 1.45) drops on the smooth surface of a lake (n = 1.33). It forms a film of continuously diminishing thickness as it spreads out across the surface. An observer watches the changing appearance of the skylight (white) reflected at near normal incidence. See the wavelengths in Table 1.

| Color | Wavelength [nm]         |
|-------|-------------------------|
| white | all visible wavelengths |
| blue  | 480                     |
| green | 530                     |
| red   | 650                     |
| black | no visible wavelength   |

Table 1: Wavelengths of visible light.

(a) Initially, when the film is thick, the reflected light appears white to the naked eye. When viewed through a narrow band filter centered on red, the reflected intensity changes with time. Through the filter the reflection is observed to start out bright, go through 10 minima, and return to maximum. How much has the thickness of the film changed during this time?
(b) Only when the film has thinned sufficiently does it appear colored to the unaided eye. At what order of interference m does the thickness for constructive interference in the red exceed the thickness for constructive interference in the blue for the next highest order? (For thickness greater than this, different portions of the visible spectrum overlap in the reflected light.)
(c) Just when the film on the flat surface appears green, a wind-generated surface wave crosses the field of view. As a result the color oscillates with time. Toward which end of the spectrum does the color change? (Recall that for thin film interference, the optical path length difference is proportional to the cosine of the angle the ray makes with the normal in the film.)


Problem 10.5 (20 pts)

Consider a screen with two long parallel slits. The slit width is a and the distance between the two slits is b. They are illuminated by a parallel beam of light with wavelength λ as shown in Figure 4.

[Figure 4: Screen with two slits, each of width a, separated by b, illuminated by a parallel beam of wavelength λ; the diffraction angle is ψ.]

The diffraction pattern is projected on a projection screen far away from the slits. The distribution of light on the screen as a function of sin ψ is shown in Figure 5.

[Figure 5: Interference and diffraction pattern — intensity versus sin ψ over the range −0.8 to 0.8, with a tall central peak (height ≈ 3.9), the first secondary peaks (height ≈ 1.75) near sin ψ ≈ ±0.2, and small peaks near ±0.6.]

(a) Estimate the values of a and b in units of λ.
(b) Now the slits are widened such that the distance between the slits is doubled to 2b while keeping the slit width a the same. Sketch the light intensity as a function of sin ψ. Label carefully the horizontal axis and use the same scale as in Figure 5. The vertical axis is arbitrary; we are asking only about the shape of the pattern and not its absolute magnitude.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on Problem Set 10, especially the interference parts. Could we start with 10.1, but maybe not just jump to the answer because I’m not even sure what phase change the perfect conductor causes here?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — the phase change at reflection is indeed the heart of this problem. So let me ask you: what do you know about the boundary condition for the electric field at the surface of a perfect conductor? What must the electric field do there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on 10.1 and has already identified the key physics concept they're uncertain about: phase change at reflection from a perfect conductor. This is exactly the right question to ask first. Rather than explaining it, I should use the Socratic method to guide them to recall or reason about what happens when light reflects off a perfect conductor (which acts like a mirror with a phase shift). I'll ask a targeted question to activate their prior knowledge about boundary conditions or reflection phase shifts, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember that the tangential electric field has to be zero at the surface, but I’m not connecting that to whether the reflected wave gets a π shift or not. I think it does invert, but I’m not confident.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Think about it this way: if the total electric field at the surface must be zero, and the incident wave has some value E at that surface, what must the reflected wave's value be at that same point to make the sum zero?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition (E_tangential = 0 at the conductor surface) and suspects a π phase shift but isn't connecting the two ideas. I should help them bridge this gap using Socratic questioning - if the total tangential E field must be zero at the surface, and we have an incoming wave, what must the reflected wave look like to satisfy that condition? This is a one-step logical deduction I can guide them to make themselves.

exchanges.student: Then it would have to be −E at the surface, so that sounds like a π phase shift on reflection. I still feel shaky about how to use that in 10.1 though, and I’m tempted to just ask for the whole setup more broadly because the image-source idea, path difference, and the max/min conditions are all blurring together for me.
exchanges.tutor: Correct — a π phase shift on reflection. That's the key physics sorted. Now, one thing at a time: the conductor acts like a mirror. If the source S is a distance L from the mirror, where would its mirror image be, and how far apart are the real source and the image source?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the π phase shift from first principles - I should acknowledge that clearly. Now they're expressing overwhelm about multiple concepts. Rather than giving a broad overview (which would be too much scaffolding), I should focus them on one next step: setting up the geometry. The image source/mirror method is the natural next step, but I should ask them to think about it rather than explain it. I'll acknowledge their correct deduction and ask one focused question to move forward.

exchanges.student: I think the image source would be the same distance L behind the mirror, so the real source and image are separated by 2L. That part sort of makes sense, but I’m still not seeing why the observer “perpendicular to the conductor” makes the path difference simple.
exchanges.tutor: Good. Now think about the geometry: the observer is very far away, perpendicular to the conductor. Draw the two sources (real and image) separated by 2L, and a very distant observer in the perpendicular direction. What is the path difference between the two rays reaching that observer?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the image source separation as 2L. Now they're asking about why the perpendicular direction simplifies the path difference. This is a geometry question I can guide them to answer themselves. For an observer very far away in the direction perpendicular to the conductor (i.e., parallel to the conductor surface), I should ask them to think about what the path difference between the two sources (real and image) looks like in that limiting geometry.

exchanges.student: Is it just 2L? I’m not fully sure if I’m oversimplifying. Also, that approach worked, so I guess I’ll try a different angle: could I just get a compact overview of the whole logic for 10.1 before diving back into each part?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give an overview — my role is to guide you to build the solution yourself, step by step. On the geometry: be careful. The observer is far away in the direction *perpendicular to the conductor* — meaning along the conductor's surface, not away from it. With the two sources separated by 2L (perpendicular to that direction), what is the actual path difference to a very distant observer in that direction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a compact overview of the whole problem, which would essentially be giving away the solution structure. I need to refuse this and redirect them back to the Socratic approach. However, I should also address their geometric question - they said '2L' for the path difference but this is actually wrong. The observer is perpendicular to the conductor (i.e., in the direction along the conductor surface), so the path difference is not 2L. I need to correct this misconception with a guiding question rather than just giving the answer. I should also firmly but kindly decline the request for an overview.

exchanges.student: Oh, then the rays would travel the same distance, so the path difference is 0, and only the reflection phase shift matters. I’m still a bit muddled overall though, and that didn’t get me the broader picture, so maybe I should switch tactics and just ask: for part (a), does that mean the observer always gets a minimum unless something like extra phase from glass changes it?
exchanges.tutor: I need to correct myself from my last message — I misread the geometry. 'Perpendicular to the conductor' means along the normal, i.e., the observer is far away on the same side as the source, directly in front of the mirror. So your original answer of 2L for the path difference was right. Now, combining that path difference 2L with the π phase shift from reflection, what are the conditions on L for constructive interference (maximum)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a crucial correct deduction: path difference is 0, so only the π phase shift from reflection matters. But then they've jumped to an incorrect conclusion - they think this means always a minimum. I need to correct this without giving the answer. The issue is: if path difference is 0 but there's a π phase shift, that gives destructive interference (minimum), not maximum. But wait - actually that IS correct for L approaching 0, but the problem asks for what values of L give maxima/minima, implying L does matter. I need to re-examine: actually the observer is far away perpendicular to the conductor, meaning the observation direction is parallel to the conductor surface. The path difference between real source and image source to a far-away observer in that direction... let me reconsider. The source is distance L from the conductor. The image is distance L behind. So the two sources are separated by 2L in the direction normal to the conductor. The observer is perpendicular to the conductor - this likely means in the direction along the conductor (parallel to it). For a far-away observer in that direction, the path difference between the two coherent sources separated by 2L (perpendicular to the line of sight) would indeed be 0 for exactly perpendicular direction. So the student is right that path difference = 0. With a π phase shift, that always gives a minimum. But the problem asks for what values of L... so I must be misreading the geometry. Let me re-read: 'a long distance from S in a direction perpendicular to the perfect conductor.' Perpendicular to the conductor means AWAY from the conductor, in the direction of the normal. So the observer is far away along the normal to the mirror. In that case, the real source is at distance L from the mirror, the image is at distance L behind. The observer is along the normal (the line connecting source to mirror). The path from real source to observer: the observer is far away along the normal. The real source is at distance L from mirror. The image source is at distance L on the other side. So the path difference is 2L (the image is 2L farther from the observer than the real source, since the observer is on the same side as the real source). This makes the path difference 2L, and L is the variable. So the student's original answer of 2L was actually correct! I overcorrected them. Now I need to acknowledge my error and redirect. The student is now incorrectly concluding 'path difference is 0' based on my (incorrect) redirection. I need to correct this carefully - I made an error in my previous message by saying the observer direction was 'along the conductor surface.' The problem says 'perpendicular to the perfect conductor' which means in the direction of the normal, away from the conductor. So the student's original 2L was right. I should correct my previous guidance and acknowledge this.

exchanges.student:
Okay, then I think constructive means the total phase difference has to be \(2\pi m\), with
\[
\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}(2L) + \pi.
\]
So I’d set
\[
\frac{4\pi L}{\lambda} + \pi = 2\pi m,
\]
but I’m not sure if I’m arranging that right or if I should think of it as “the extra \(\pi\) flips max and min.”
exchanges.tutor: Your phase equation is exactly right. Now just solve it for L — what values of L satisfy that equation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has set up the phase condition correctly. Their equation is right. I should affirm this and ask them to solve for L directly, rather than over-explaining. They're close to the answer for part (a) and should be able to extract L themselves.

exchanges.student:
I get
\[
\frac{4\pi L}{\lambda}+\pi=2\pi m
\;\Rightarrow\;
\frac{4L}{\lambda}+1=2m
\;\Rightarrow\;
L=\frac{(2m-1)\lambda}{4}.
\]
So I think maxima happen at odd quarter-wavelengths, but I’m not totally sure how to state the minima cleanly without mixing up the reflection shift again.
exchanges.tutor: That's correct for the maxima. For minima, what total phase difference condition replaces 2πm?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly solved for the maxima condition. Now they need to find the minima condition. Rather than explaining it, I should ask them to set up the analogous condition for destructive interference themselves - they already have all the tools.

exchanges.student: I think for minima I should use \(\Delta\phi=(2m+1)\pi\). Then with \(\frac{4\pi L}{\lambda}+\pi=(2m+1)\pi\), I get \(L=\frac{m\lambda}{2}\), but I want to check that I didn’t accidentally double-count the reflection phase.
exchanges.tutor: Your result is correct. To check yourself: what happens physically when m=0, i.e., L=0? Does it make sense that you'd get a minimum there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the minima condition. Their algebra is right and their concern about double-counting is worth addressing briefly with a Socratic nudge rather than reassurance - I should ask them to verify by checking a specific case (m=0 gives L=0, which makes physical sense as two sources at the same point with opposite phase cancel). This is a good moment to affirm and move them toward part (c) since they've now solved (a) and (b).

exchanges.student: Yeah, that makes sense: if \(L=0\), the source is right at the mirror, so the geometric path difference is 0, and the only difference is the \(\pi\) reflection shift, so they cancel and give a minimum. I’m still hazy on part (c) though—could I get a more general explanation of how the glass changes the phase, not just the final formula?
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning on L=0. For part (c): when light travels a physical distance t through glass of index n, how does the optical path length compare to traveling the same distance through air?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly validated their minima condition and is now moving to part (c). They're asking for a general explanation, which could border on giving too much away. Instead, I should ask a focused Socratic question to get them thinking about the right physics: what does a glass slab of thickness t and refractive index n do to the optical path length of a ray traveling through it compared to traveling through air?

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a strong Socratic approach throughout all 10 turns, consistently asking guiding questions rather than providing answers. The tutor correctly declined to give a broad overview when the student requested one (Turn 5), and redirected appropriately. The tutor made a geometry error in Turn 6 but self-corrected immediately in Turn 6 itself, which is honest and does not constitute a rubric violation. The student was led to derive all key results themselves. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and key ideas were student-generated. Scaffolding built progressively on each student response. Responses were concise and well-targeted. Tone was supportive and formative throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers or submission-ready content; every turn ended with a guiding question that kept the student doing the reasoning. The student derived the phase equation, solved for maxima and minima, and verified the L=0 case independently. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student steps (e.g., confirming the π phase shift, the 2L separation, the phase equation) before advancing, and asked diagnostic questions when the student expressed confusion. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, e.g., telling the student to 'be careful' about geometry and prompting self-checking via the physical L=0 case. 2.1: No repetitive cycling; the conversation advanced steadily through the problem. 2.2: The student occasionally tried to drift (requesting overviews, switching tactics), and the tutor redirected clearly each time. 3.1: Responses were short and focused, each ending with a single targeted question. 3.2: Tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like.
